text: to summarize my views on the film i think beyond focusing on committed jazz players what whiplash and la la land really have in common is how they portray the pursuit of greatness from chazelles perspective its not difficult to achieve success in that most people dont have the capacity for the talent or the work those with the force of will are eventually able to push the society around them to bend to their will and accept their work its true with andrew neiman its true with mia dolan and sebastian wilder the hardship they face isnt their lack of ability or skill its what they sacrifice in that pursuit what is common is the toxicity of their pursuit towards those around them andrew becomes arrogant and throws away his girlfriend because of his goals he literally bleeds for his work mia and sebastian can either both follow their dreams or one has to give partway so they can be together whats illuminating is that it is never a discussion that both might compromise on their goals both are so dedicated on their paths they would never be willing to concede any ground they do damage to their partners because they romanticize success more than they do their romance la la land perfectly deconstructs the entire concept of ill always love you with its ending they will always love each other and they both know it theyre not rick and ilsa they do have the choice to be together but they didnt i think what damien chazelle says about how you get a sort of tunnel vision in your pursuit of greatness where you forget about the people you care about is something powerful how his protagonists push themselves past their limitations to sometimes dangerous degrees is a testament to how toxic their lives can be its fascinating i literally have not and will not ever see a movie better than this we really do not deserve movies this good jacques demy for the digital age walks the uneasy bridge between romance and reality almost as nimbly as singin in the rain did the one between silent films and talkiesi think this is a more difficult and more rewarding film than some people are expecting or will be interested in seeing the way it disintegrates from the technicolor to it took me a while to parse out how i felt about blade runner 2049 as someone who didnt like blade runner og 2049 was on a whole different level heres a link to my full review with all kinds of formatting and whatnot ridley scotts 1982 cult hit blade runner is often credited with a great many introductions its often described as the first mainstream cyberpunk aesthetic put to film the countless films and media it has influenced on its stylistic grit and fashion alone are many discussions in and of themselves while earlier short stories have touched upon the disparaging slums of the future and painted them as technological outcasts none imparted a unified vision quite like blade runner ryan gosling wife adi love hard scifi thought provoking films and i cherish tension and tone when i watched blade runner years back i recall not enjoying it much like the early critics and audiences that were exposed to the film in theaters in the early 80s it wasnt until much later that it received its cult status and respect i havent gone back to explore the movie since feeling disappointed by it many moons back but i was quite excited about blade runner 2049 not only was it obvious that gosling ford and vileenveie were a powerhouse team but each and every trailer was art in motion a scifi fever dream 15 minutes into the movie i was already in awe blade runner 2049 is upon first impressions from this scifi lover a masterpiece straight up i couldnt get over the craftsmenship of the film and the attention to detail in every single scene the movie is rich vivid color officerunning at nearly 3 hours long there wasnt a single time that it dragged for me not a single feeling of slow pace among the lengthy and prolonged scenes that built on tone and world more than plot advancement there are abundant scenes that exist purely to craft the world immerse you in a neoncaked la cityscape douse you with ambient and overpowering music and make you feel the film flows from plot development and following a detective lead noir style to building a romantic connection between gosling and his consumerbought ai girlfriend as the plot progresses and the lead builds the next scene adds to the lushness of the world in a world of so much stimulation its unclear who is human and what it even means to be human i found myself never really sure of the motivations behind each character whether they were synthetic selfaware or the rules they played by each scene dropped more hints more clues and more strands to follow as to how the world worked beyond the alreadycomplex main plot there were enough sidestories to fill a miniseries that i would gladly watch and gladly discuss at length i love science fiction i believe that movies have the ability to inspire their audience to achieve greater things and build a world that they may want to see when its a dark scifi flick like blade runner it doesnt even much matter to me that it sours most displays of technological progress with sorrow and pain whether its a probable human pain or a synthetized and designed pain the catharsis exists for us and it really should make us question the morality of the film is so grayscale that i would be hardpressed to make any claims about who is a villain and who is a hero letos character commands each moment onscreen and purposefully disturbs with his violent nonchalance but his achievements fall under the umbrella of beneficence there is enough interesting shit on screen and inworld in this film that it can provide hours of discussion for those that have a desire to carry on with heavy mental lifting and thats not even the main plot alone which requires actual focus and attention to parse even in the end our 4 person viewing party talked for over an hour throwing out clues and hints and moments that stood out as evidence to some bewildering theory we thought the movie was casting upon usthis is definitely a movie one should watch twice and i would very much love to the frames themselves this movie is pure eyecandy if you havent peeped the cyberpunk aesthetic before and the concept of a grimy striving future appeals to you you might find this movie to be art in motion its gorgeous in every scene the shots of hovercars cruising through the city coast you through a woozy simulation of actual flight the replicants are portrayed as relatably human and bizarre weird and robotic human yet not ever quite human with this portrayal comes a beauty in the acting the pain on display and the movie makes us wallow in it in every death and my god some of these deaths are insanely visceral one of the most tense climaxes ive ever seen is on display here in every gutwrenching realization or recognition of mistake or foolishness that our characters reveal to themselves there feels like a nuance and contemplation on display that these actors masterfully depict perhaps its because of the artistic merit the tone the lushness of the world or the acting itself but i found myself so much more sentimental of plot development than i do in most movies the plot isnt a doomsday scenario or immediately threatening to this world but it maintains a sense of weight and it constantly builds on its scifi world by layering scene after scene of thought provoking content clone roomive long held the opinion that scifi has the ability to create intrigue merely through images and world building just seeing future tech or a snippet of how society works gets my gears turning and trying to piece together the way this world works this directors slower pace in his films tends to lend itself the best to scifi and movies with lush world to movies where the environment is a character itself where moments in villeneuves sicario tense but slow blade runners genre crafted much more value in each slow moment even his latest effort arrival while scifi isnt worldbuilding or propelling the lore of the world in most scenes that the aliens arent on screen or being analyzed blade runner however is generating detail every single second its depicting the world because it is so far into the future and so lush ins depictions of the world this is the exact reason that while others have claimed this movie felt slow i found it constantly gripping not only is it atmospheric throughout its showing characters or environments that are always interesting to analyze i had similar experiences while watching westworld what makes a masterpiece what a lofty question right how can one determine quality in a primarily subjective medium more ways than one thats for sure i look back on this film and ask myself what could it have done better i dont feel that adding gratuitous nudity gore or different dialogue would have aided the core of the film it stands to reason that there isnt anything this film needed to amplify the feelings and emotions it already evoked then maybe its approaching perfection could it have been shorter not for me could it have had more plot development better acting more of the good stuff that ive already called out i mean sure i guess so but i didnt find myself yearning for those things because it so effectively satiated me with its runtime and visual display i do believe that its hard to identify a masterpiece from a single viewing i dont even know if calling something so lofty serves it much more than simply explaining what makes it great but you know what this movie was so exceptional and hit so smoothly on so many cylinders that i think it is damn near perfect for what it was trying to be so well realized in its own goals that it feels damn near flawless and now were left with a discourse and continued discussion of the movie a discussion that blossoms the values of the film even more were left with a desire to share in this experience a lingering desire to let this film inspire other filmmakers or inspire the general public to contemplate appreciate and think im happy leaving that here in the face of almost unobtainable expectations due to blade runner 2049 being a sequel to a beloved cult classic it manages to succeed in being a better movie than blade runner in almost every way its wellacted its visually stunning its tense and atmospheric its thoughtprovoking its intense and has plenty of scenes in a film ive never seen it makes me wonder and dream its a joy to experience for anyone that has the energy to engage and analyze ponder and try to piece together this scifi film noir i implore you peep this movie recommendations recommended for fans of this genre absolutely any fan of scifi dramas thrillers mysteries noir excellent filmmaking or thoughtprovoking spectacle you should see this movie if youve got the attention and focus to process nearly 3 hours of complex science fiction love discussing cinema or want to be moved fucking peep this jam recommended for nonfans of this genre no i dont believe that every exceptional film is recommendable to everyone some individuals merely dont have the attention to sit through slower scenes that run up to nearly 3 hours some arent going to enjoy the mental gymnastics required to understand the plot some wont find the aesthetics or the eardrum smashing music to add to the experience some folks want every single movie to be a casual affair one they can watch to vegout this isnt that movie i almost think it would be disrespectful to watch this movie with a halfasleep candor as such this feels like an exquisite film that simply isnt for all movie audiences and thats totally ok semiarbitrary score 9510 has no one else noticed how the oscars flub ecstatically reinforces the primary themes of la la land dreams deferred struggles between success and compromise failures reimagined echoes of the past shaping the future miscommunication in the capitalist zeitgeist yes warren beatty is the hollywood royalty la la land so longingly pines for but it is even more eerie when you realize how la la lands ending so closely mirrors natalie woods visit of former lover farmer beatty at the end of splendor in the grass maybe hollywood is only capable of telling one story la la land loses to moonlight but it only ever wanted the stars the rejection of ceremony is like all rebellions a fever dream of new possibilities jazz is the locution of rejection in the same way james deans death reinvigorates his existential craftsmanship with a mythos outside the frame knowing emma stone was standing on a stage with her best actress statue in one hand and then having that excessive but not inessential finality snatched away gives her mia character new perceptions into regret and possibility it all so perfectly encapsulates hollywoods dreambuilding economy that it could only be attributed to god or hitchcock while watching rebel without a cause just before the climactic scene at the griffith observatory our main lovers are prepared to kiss the reel snags and the house lights go up and they pull away they then decide to go to the griffith observatory itself and make out there its as if the film itself is only necessary to the fantasy as a projector on the canvas of imagination that is the shamanic power of dreambuilding will always exist somewhere outside the film casablanca filmed outside a starbucks vs movie posters hanging above us where we sleep so we can see now that la la land as both best picture winner and best picture loser not only mirrors the alternate timeline pastiche at the end of the film itself but restates and empowers the entirety of hollywoods thematic project reading winners off a cue card mundane reality picking names out of a hat jazz reincarnation the ending flashback seems to be entirely from mias perspective how do we know seb wanted kids what can our longing for alternate realities be but a projection of existence onto everything around us we insert our fantasia into the void assembled out of various pieces of the best part of our hopes and expectations so when the world projected itself on to the stage of the academy awards it was all there hitchcock kazan bonnie and clyde the infinite phantasmagorical rage that drives people to church crime or the theater we are as oblivious to the machinations of film making as we are to the individual neurons firing away in our dreams all we see are the iconoclasts milestone linked to milestone and the traffic and infrastructure connecting them lost in the haze of the mirage emma stone in a recent interview talks about the turmoil of anxiety have our superstars ever looked more anxious more faulty more human than when the camera zooms in on their sweaty faces during the reading of the nominees it is one of the rare times we get a look beneath the glitz and glam at the turmoil of anxiety that powers the filmmaking craft and capitalism itself what is the antidote jazz improvisation selfactivation going out and emulating movie stars or jazz icons of the past rebirthing ourselves in the lovely mysteries we pray are never deciphered my favorite scene in the film is when mia looks on in confusion and disappointment as seb plays cheesy synth for his touring band the messengers whats the message with what similar horror must the loved ones of both the moonlight and la la land cast looked on their significant others faces when the stranglehold with which the chimera holds up their lives is nakedly revealed as for us we stargazers audience to a story with multiple final reels a song never played the same way twice the theme of our lives is revealed in the turmoil when fantasy and reality clash when practicality is tempted by visions of a pink moon surrounded by the aspirations of a million stars you didnt win play it again i hope this type of post is okay the proposal for despite not being very deserving ive got a pet theory that the best way to win a best picture oscar isnt to be the best or most influential film but just to be a relatively alright movie made by a good but undecorated director in an otherwise weak year for drama my case study for this is crash 2005 by paul haggis a dreary and unsubtle exploration of race haggis movie has very few compelling characters some laughable dialogue and acting which is often only just above soap level and yet it won in a year where other contenders included brokeback mountain arguably a much more lauded and influential film now haggis isnt a famous director although he is wellknown in hollywood for his script work what worked in crashs favour is that its focus on race allowed the academy to feel progressive while ignoring the other contenders silence does a similar thing its obviously intended as some sort of magnum opus for scorsese it confronts a lot of themes running through his work and its very long and insistent something which audiences often interpret as necessary for a film to be a work of art scorsese is a much better director than haggis and silence is a better film than crash but it is definitely not a best picture winner especially in a year with moonlight la la land arrival and even manchester by the sea in the selection however it has very high chances simply because it is competently made and the academy is still smarting over giving scorsese an oscar for a remake a while ago so how do we feel about undeserving movies winning awards what do you think the chances of came on i got me thinking with all thats going on in hollywood la la land was released with perfect timing to make it successful let me start by saying that i see la la land as an impressive cinematic achievement and what im about to say shouldnt be taken as critique against the movie i feel like la la land like few other movies captures what is known to the world as classic hollywood cinema so graciously and perfectly it evokes a feeling of a long gone time for cinema with beautiful picturesque scenes stories of love making audiences dream and forget their daytoday for two hours this feeling is part of what made people love la la land so much the phrase they dont make movies like this anymore couldnt fit better here what im arguing is that had la la land dropped even just a year later while remaining a quality product the ability of it producing said emotion would have been severely weakened by the developments surrounding major hollywood figures now im not saying that would have taken away from the success of la la land but frankly that movement did start processes that lead to hollywood being seen increasingly ciritcal as an insitution fed by abuse of power bigotry and racism so in a time where a growing percentage of moviegoers has these things in mind generating a longlost passion for classical hollywood cinema could be more difficult than it was around the time la la land was released to be honest i do see this point as a positive one for la la land though in hindsight it feels like one last celebration of what hollywood standsstood for for decades before new powers and movements would begin change it as an institution which is what is increasingly beginning to happen around now what do you think about it and do you know of any other movies whose release dates were unintenionally timed really well dig it hepcats the word on the street is that a certain low life crime author jamie e has been saturating his ultraviolent penny dreadfuls with allusions to the hollywood dream machine lathering his sinfully sin tillating pulp visions in a nightmare inverted world of hollywood allusions item 1988s the big nowhere uses the description a perceptual switch to devilishly decadent widescreen technicolor as a signal that the narrator is switching to perverse flashbacks of a perverted childhood format switch in a novel what other vile visual ideas will our demon dog introduce next item same novel uses as a plot point the idea of an artsy european experimental film with gilded dogs and swarthy males in black and white film scenes to reveal the character of our hero could our jamie be taking a potshot at the fantasies of cocteau or is this a shout out to kenneth anger whose hollywood babylon would make even the readers of confidential blush all while informing our pulp dostoyevskys wicked dreams item la confidential which some of you may know as a sinfully seductive but much hacked and cut script from the novel takes americas two most wholesome panaceas dragnet and disney and fuses them into a frankensteins monster of a serial killer plot what hidden messages does evil ellroy see in american pop culture item white jazzs nonstop staccato rhythm bashes the brain while hypnotizing simultaneously acting as an analogue for a long take while leading us down an alleyway involving howard hughs film factory and the b picture horror scene taking along a dirty picture conspiracy along the way we ask our faithful readers smut historians allto give the lowdown on what b picture horrors gave off the crazy resonances that clearly ellroys mind much like they did his weirdly sympathetic serial pychos item jimmys got it in his head that hes bound for hollywood and for every pic that captures his prose like a camera stealing someones soul the swoony la confidential or rampart where the very celluloid seems as high and confused as one of his wayward heroes theres some that fail like the black dahlia or the secret plagiarism of tue detective season 2 shame we want our faithful readers to give us the skinny on the flicks that accidentally capture ellroy like the satanic conspiracies of the seventh victim the overdone pop culture stews of the big combo and blast of silence or the mix of tawdry glamour and violence of the witch who came from the sea which plays like a lost film from one of his books any more cinematic dirt on this criminal mastermind and remember this is all off the record on the qt and very hush hush la la land 2016 is a romantic musical about two aspiring artists mia and sebastian struggling to follow their dreams of stardom in los angeles while unexpectedly falling in love with each other director damien chazelle production designers david wasco and sandy reynoldswasco throughout the entirety of la la land 2016 the concept of kinesis specifically camera movement is used to create mood and express emotion in the very first scene another day of sun the camera travels through busy traffic while people sing and dance on their vehicles to establish an initial sense of excitement and restlessness this is crucial as it presents the idea of dreamers trying to reach their potential because of the cameras busy movement the audience is already made aware of the storys main conflict without even being introduced to either of the main characters because the majority of the film uses this style of camera movement the audience adapts and accepts it as normal and so when the camera stops moving it becomes blatantly apparent the director damien chazelle took advantage of this and significantly reduced the movement of the camera during the films most important moments to emphasize the intended emotions in one of the films most pivotal scenes audition mia sings for a chance to reach her dream of becoming an actress and to change her life forever for the duration of the scene aside from a zoom in dolly shot and zoom out the camera stays focused on mia in a closeup shot because of the movement throughout the film this scene has a powerful effect on the audience which helps them understand the story on a much deeper level another day of sun audition la la land makes me want to be alive for one hour and fifty minutes then sebastian says welcome to sebs and it makes me want to be dead i enjoy the 2016 damien chazelle filmla la land i want to take some time to talk about the epilogue of the filmive heard many different theories about what the epilogue truly means the reality is the idea that true love is being willing to make sacrifices in this epilogue sebastian was far more willing to make sacrifices for mia than she was for him there is a scene earlier on in the film that beautifully foreshadows the entire story the scene after sebastian adulthood and childhood just in different ways and the entire film is kind of an exploration of that dichotomy once again there has been much worse depicted in film before plus its a movie none of it is real gary and alana are fictional this movie to me lives in a version of reality similar to la la land it just seems like in the last decade peoples media literacy has been dumbed down to remedial levels people put movies into three categories 1 franchise movies which cant be criticized because its just a fun movie 2 childrens movies which cant be criticized because its just a kid movie and 3 serious movies that must unambiguously perfect from a moralethical standpoint before it can even be considered consumable much less a good piece of art because for some reason theres a staggering amount of people of people who are unable to separate a movie from reality its just exhausting anyways the movie is fantastic paul thomas anderson has created another masterpiece and its a shame embrace the looseness of it i am 14 years old i would love to see what u guys think of my favorite movies 1 jojo rabbit 2019 2 moonrise kingdom 2012 3 the perks of being a wallflower 2012 4 the shining 1980 5 the empire strikes back 1980 6 aliens 1986 7 10 cloverfield lane 2016 8 2001 a space odyssey 1968 9 ferris buellers day off 1986 10 fantastic mr fox 2009 11 cloverfield 2008 12 pans labyrinth 2006 13 la la land 2016 14 moon 2009 15 vertigo 1958 16 the village 2004 17 hunt for the wilderpeople 2016 18 tenet 2020 19 seven samurai 1954 20 pet sematary 2019 spoilers ahead watched through itunes streaming in the current state of the world where a pandemic rages and climate change is starting to upend life on this planet it is inspirational to watch films that demonstrate the ingenuity and potential of humankind to solve its problems at least when it wants to first man damien chazelles ambitious drama about neil armstrongs journey to the moon is that type of movie we all know that neil armstrong was the first to walk on the moon and many of us know the story of how he got there but its a testament to chazelle and writer josh singers talents to still manage to keep me compelled and on the edge of my seat throughout this film ryan gosling and especially claire foys strong performances the direction of this movie the films screenplay by josh singer based on james r hansens biography of the same name and justin hurwitzs score come together beautifully to create one of 2018s best films first man traces the story of astronaut neil armstrong ryan gosling and his successful apollo 11 mission to the moon in july 1969 the film portrays how armstrongs near obsessive commitment to getting to the moon impacted his family life and particularly his relationship with his wife janet claire foy the opening flying sequence and the two lunar missions can only be described as gutchurning intensity i did not watch this movie in a theater and i deeply regret not seeing it on a 100foot imax screen especially the climactic moon landing which was shot specifically for imax however even on the small screen these action sequences are breath taking what makes them so is chazelles use of extreme closeups of the astronauts and of the interiors of the aircraft and spacecraft they were piloting and an effective use of sound design the effect you get is claustrophobic and you get a sense that these pilots and astronauts who sat in these cramped quarters were completely at the whim of mother nature these sequences felt dangerous and even nightmarish first man shows a deglamourized version of armstrongs story after his daughter dies from cancer and armstrong joins the gemini astronaut program armstrongs relationship with his wife and two sons is far from perfect armstrong represents the stereotypical image of the midcentury american man stoic strong and workfocused ryan gosling portrays the american hero as a quiet and shy person who keeps his emotions to himself and doesnt usually have much to say gosling seems to have perfected this type of character in this and other films blade runner 2049 drive etc as much as i enjoyed goslings performance it is claire foy as janet armstrong who steals this film many biopics of famous men relegate the actress to play the sidelined doting wife who just sits in the background not here janet not only supports armstrong but she also challenges him to be a good and responsible father and husband in one of the best scenes in the film janet confronts neil for his reluctance to sit his sons down and explain the risks he will face on going to the moon after directing and writing three musicals guy and madeline on a park bench whiplash and la la land i was a bit surprised by damien chazelles decision to make this film and skeptical that he would pull it off the script by josh singer spotlight and the post is a major factor in the overall pacing and strength of this film but chazelles visual storytelling is also very impressive here although he has in the past relied a lot on steadicam shots kinetic editing and hyperrealism in his films chazelle does the opposite here chazelle instead relies on handheld shots which prove to be quite effective in the flight sequences with the sound design mixed in and with the use of a variety of film stocks 16mm 35mm and imax 65mm chazelle imbues this film with a continuous sense of anxiety and excitement while also effectively creating somber and intimate moments like when armstrongs daughter dies from cancer and when he reacts to his friends dying in the course of preparing to fly to the moon unlike apollo 13 first man does not hold nasas and armstrongs accomplishments into space in complete reverence or somehow mythologize what we did no doubt the film applauds the efforts of all involved who took us to the moon but the filmmakers also comment on the fact that our government was spending millions of dollars to basically send a bunch of white guys to the moon while the country was roiled in the vietnam war and dealing with social unrest at home the film also comments on the detrimental impact that these mens commitments and risks had on their families first man is a finely crafted film with great performances particularly from claire foy direction storytelling and one of the best film scores of 2018 i highly recommend you see this current state of the world where a pandemic rages and climate change is starting to upend life on this planet it is inspirational to watch films that demonstrate the ingenuity and potential of humankind to solve its problems at least when it wants to first man damien chazelles ambitious drama about neil armstrongs journey to the moon is that type of movie we all know that neil armstrong was the first to walk on the moon and many of us know the story of how he got there but its a testament to chazelle and writer josh singers talents to still manage to keep me compelled and on the edge of my seat throughout this film ryan gosling and especially claire foys strong performances the direction of this movie the films screenplay by josh singer based on james r hansens biography of the same name and justin hurwitzs score come together beautifully to create one of 2018s best films first man traces the story of astronaut neil armstrong ryan gosling and his successful apollo 11 mission to the moon in july 1969 the film portrays how armstrongs near obsessive commitment to getting to the moon impacted his family life and particularly his relationship with his wife janet claire foy the opening flying sequence and the two lunar missions can only be described as gutchurning intensity i did not watch this movie in a theater and i deeply regret not seeing it on a 100foot imax screen especially the climactic moon landing which was shot specifically for imax however even on the small screen these action sequences are breath taking what makes them so is chazelles use of extreme closeups of the astronauts and of the interiors of the aircraft and spacecraft they were piloting and an effective use of sound design the effect you get is claustrophobic and you get a sense that these pilots and astronauts who sat in these cramped quarters were completely at the whim of mother nature these sequences felt dangerous and even nightmarish first man shows a deglamourized version of armstrongs story after his daughter dies from cancer and armstrong joins the gemini astronaut program armstrongs relationship with his wife and two sons is far from perfect armstrong represents the stereotypical image of the midcentury american man stoic strong and workfocused ryan gosling portrays the american hero as a quiet and shy person who keeps his emotions to himself and doesnt usually have much to say gosling seems to have perfected this type of character in this and other films blade runner 2049 drive etc as much as i enjoyed goslings performance it is claire foy as janet armstrong who steals this film many biopics of famous men relegate the actress to play the sidelined doting wife who just sits in the background not here janet not only supports armstrong but she also challenges him to be a good and responsible father and husband in one of the best scenes in the film janet confronts neil for his reluctance to sit his sons down and explain the risks he will face on going to the moon after directing and writing three musicals guy and madeline on a park bench whiplash and la la land i was a bit surprised by damien chazelles decision to make this film and skeptical that he would pull it off the script by josh singer spotlight and the post is a major factor in the overall pacing and strength of this film but chazelles visual storytelling is also very impressive here although he has in the past relied a lot on steadicam shots kinetic editing and hyperrealism in his films chazelle does the opposite here chazelle instead relies on handheld shots which prove to be quite effective in the flight sequences with the sound design mixed in and with the use of a variety of film stocks 16mm 35mm and imax 65mm chazelle imbues this film with a continuous sense of anxiety and excitement while also effectively creating somber and intimate moments like when armstrongs daughter dies from cancer and when he reacts to his friends dying in the course of preparing to fly to the moon unlike apollo 13 first man does not hold nasas and armstrongs accomplishments into space in complete reverence or somehow mythologize what we did no doubt the film applauds the efforts of all involved who took us to the moon but the filmmakers also comment on the fact that our government was spending millions of dollars to basically send a bunch of white guys to the moon while the country was roiled in the vietnam war and dealing with social unrest at home the film also comments on the detrimental impact that these mens commitments and risks had on their families first man is a finely crafted film with great performances particularly from claire foy direction storytelling and one of the best film scores of 2018 i highly recommend you see this even if it wont be on an imax my whole point is that nothing is being revealed and thats the genius of this film if there is a genius there is camera a which shoots various actors essential live audition tape of some campy ironic adjective given that a big part of the project is to break through the artificiality of acting marriage dispute scene there is camera b a sort of inbetween camera filming a documentary of the filming of these live auditions in practice serving a similar purpose to both the camera its filming and the camera that its being filmed by that would be camera c filming a documentary about the filming of the documentary about the filming of the live auditions which offers the crucial distance needed to see the whole project for what it is a big nothingness then the crew befuddled by the pointlessness of this project start filming their behindthescenes chats adding another stratum of potential direction and intention wherein they attempt to project meaning unto a purposeless film and unto the either aimless or comicgenius director same as we do but there also comes a moment when one of them starts talking about these various levels of reality which the film layers endlessly and how the audience cant even know whether the crew isnt also faking it and are actually under total command of the director upon which any notion of what the film is abouta layered documentary about finding new acting styles about acting in general a layered metafictional documentary of the levels of reality not only wrt to film but to reality itself a documentary about the figuring out of what the documentary is about a big improvisation à la cinematic free jazz a film about the artistic endeavor in general a document on the evolution of the artistic process into a more democratic function in line with contemporaneous hippie ideals entropy etcessentially dissolves and all that were left with is perhaps the single most hilarious cinematic shaggy dog story ever told which is not to say that all the things mentioned become irrelevant since the very nature of the film is such that its shaped by whatever input the crew cast and extraneous elements happen to throw its way its just that theyre not leading to an allsatisfying grand climax but trust me when i say that we get something even better not only is this just so hilarious but that french dude at the end talk about serendipity what a magnificently digressive way to end it upping the comedy while making way for unexpectedly stirring political and personal sentiment either william greaves is a genius and this is one big hoax and joke that only he knew about the ultimate troll film of not so much the audience though that too especially if those crew talks are fake but mostly his own crew a giant dupe that nonetheless envelopes endless humanity and thematic prisms within its borders or he was in way over his head and simply stumbled into one of the greatest cinematic pranks ever made an exercise in well empty exercise in which case he was still smart enough to see what he had either way this is one of the funniest most fun things to ever be taped which is sort of the meaning of the title according to wikipedia the title came from greavess own fascination with scientific concepts the concept of symbiotaxiplasm originated from arthur f bentley in his book inquiry into inquiries essays in social theory which greaves described as those events that transpire in the course of anyones life that have an impact on the consciousness and the psyche of the average human being and how that human being also controls or effects changes or has an impact on the environment for all those who have not seen the film and may be confused by this post know that there is no way to describe the film since it spielberg kaminski cinematographer and stockhausen production designer and critical of them and wonder what others think overall i didnt really care for the film but the thing that bothered me the most against expectations  was the basic look of it the wise version from the 50s which is like a c with very high highs in my book has a very distinctive technicolor look to it which spectacularizes and theatricalizes a social text about immigration race gentrification and the lives of the poor its fascinating historically as a bigbudget musical about the lives of the poor spielberg doesnt radically change the approach staying in the mode of homage he decided to go with this ultraslick cinemascope look where there are identifiable primary colors everywhere but theyve been slightly desaturated or browned in some way almost going with a sort of sepiacoated nostalgia every setting in this is lit to within an inch of its life especially in the gymnasium dance where dozens of studio lights are apparently visible to create extreme contrast effects the disney 4k uhd was great for testing the capabilities of my lg c1 oled lens flares shininess etc cool is moved to the pier and they go for like saharadesert diffused sunlight whereas wise chose to go with headlights of trucks which sells the kind of chaos or triage feel of the number coming after the rumble also a better placement decision it seems like this adaptation is splitting the difference between colorful dream world and a kind of sharp realistic hollywood production value even more distracting is that occasionally this looked like a slightly desaturated la la land sometimes things looked too good to the point where it was fake which im not always against and yet sometimes it also seemed like they were going for that effect ultimately because of the aesthetic disunity of the blended stage production and real world look i found the extreme portraitstudio lighting color grading and camera movement to be very distracting and not purposeful or maybe im missing what the purpose is i actually think the dancing wouldve come through better with more cuts and more static shots i think the reason these craft choices stand out to me the most as the main problem of the film is that i initially had the feeling that a new adaptation of west side story was going to be a hard sell unless the libretto was aggressively changed by kushner wss has a facile outlook on issues of immigration and race at its core and is largely carried by bernstein sondheim and robbins the wise version gets by on the strength of having some very exquisitely shot numbers but its not a profound film this adaptation feels too aesthetically reverent to the source material its very classically shot when i think the only way to approach a new adaptation of a dated text like this is with a radical reimagining which wouldve involved a lot more aesthetic risk on the side of production design cinematography etc thats not spielbergs game of course so we revert to marketable nostalgia beyond the fact the new screenplay doesnt do much to make the story more compelling i think it needed to either be more dreamlike or more realistic to compellingly reframe whats going on i would say even some of the decisions on how to shoot the choreography felt undoubtedly bigger and i like big excessive musical numbers with lots of extras big apologist for hello dolly and yet less immersive too much to focus on harder to really see the dancing too pretty i have a lot of questions about whether or not wss can ever be adapted well but i think a good starting place would be for a director to be more thoughtful basically about how to aestheticize conditions of poverty and discrimination that would start with a better screenplay and would involve a more challenging less markettested blockbuster look it was tough for me watching this initially i had been skeptical that a new adaptation from spielberg could work but he is such blocking moves tracking shots etc that had a cool factor to them but theyd always land in the end on uninteresting subject matter oh last little pet peeve anyone notice that the sound recording on the vocals in this was super compressed i was ready to be swept up in the sentimentality but the acting and singing of ziegler and debose was undercut by the way their vocals became almost comically robotic through unnecessary pitchcorrection and compression what the hell arent the characters in this supposed to be experiencing super heightened emotions wouldnt a little pitchyness or wavering dynamics be important to conveying that once upon a time down in guadalajara mexico a boy was born as he grew the boy took an interest in the arts and with his fathers super 8 camera he started to produce little short stories using action figures and potatoes this passion blossomed into an obsession channeled both into his education and his career as director he created a plethora of short films while also working on the mexican show la hora marcada on the side he honed his skills as a special effects artist and formed his own fx company necropia but all the while the ambition to create bigger and better things loomed he needed to achieve his childhood dreams and finally create his first feature length film and so in 1993 after years of hard work on what wound up being mexicos highest budgeted film up to that point guillermo del toro released his debut cronos video essay version with clips cronos is a very assured film especially for a feature debut with a great attention for detail impressively it already feels like what we know and understand a del toro picture to be a blend of classic and modern horror deeply rooted in historical lore but also brimming with fairytale qualities with a peculiar interest in childhood wonderment institutional villainy clockwork and creepy crawlies the film also begins in typical fashion just like his later movies such as the devils backbone and the shape of water cronos opens with a ministory told via curious voiceover narration and gliding camerawork it immediately sets up a confounding mystery that will serve as the beating heart of this strange film and introduces audiences to the cronos device named after the greek god of time in presentday mexico we meet our protagonist jesus played by recurring del toro favourite federico luppi jesus runs his own antique shop but otherwise lives a very subdued life alongside his wife and young granddaughter aurora his days start to become exciting as several shadowy figures start to take an interest in a certain statue among his wares one of these men is played by ron perlman who of course became another mainstay in the del toro playbook del toro requested perlmans involvement in the film by writing a personalised letter detailing all the aspects he admired in the actor perlman was naturally flattered and agreed to take part despite not speaking any spanish resulting in his character being rewritten as a bumbling american expat even in english though he was still victim to some unfortunate adr doesnt matter the pair got on like a haunted mansion on fire with perlman taking a huge paycut when the budget got out of hand returning to the plot jesus himself is lured towards the item as cockroaches spill out of its open eye cavity inside the statue he and his granddaughter discover the cronos device which springs into action and embeds itself into his flesh relatable similar thing happened to me with a binder clip basically the cronos device actually houses a disturbing old bug whose sting can grant you eternal life downside is it does so by turning you into a vampire of sorts jesus becomes crippingly addicted to the youthful vigour the stabbystabby bug box provides him and causes him to shave off his grandfatherly moustache effectively shaving the years off his face and morphing him into mexican rhys ifans you know how these things go he also discovers a need to drink human blood and all that and after apparently dying jesus lives up to his namesake and resurrects as a creeping undead figure with marble skin bloody heck bloody neck anyway there is also an evil terminally ill business fiend trying to get his hands on the cronos device to prolong his own existence jesus cant allow this guy or his lackey nephew perlman to succeed nor can he stomach his own suffering situation leading to a beautifully bittersweet ending with del toro proving for the first of many times that horror doesnt always just have to be blood and guts and nihilism the serious themes are great of course but guillermo del toro also knows how to balance it out with some humour and levity perlman is a regular source of fun a subplot involving his obsession with plastic surgery results in a great recurring gag the black banter of the funeral workers is a welcome sidetrack from the main event leading to the characters returning in the 2010 film we are what we are overall this is just a tribute to del toros skills as writer director and effects artist the story and the characters connect on both the horror and drama levels as required the camera always seems to be in the right place the tone is deadon everything was already in place so early on a natural talent through and through i got the chance to see a restored version of nights of cabiria in theaters a few days ago it was my third viewing of the film but i think i am now fully convinced it is fellinis best and it is definitely my favorite of his ive seen almost all of fellinis other films and he certainly has some other incredible masterpieces but to me nights of cabiria is the perfect combination of his neorealist roots and his fascination with dreams and fantastical imagery la strada is a movie made in a similar vain but i would say giullieta masinas performance in nights of cabiria elevates it to another level its the perfect coming together of performance direction cinematography sadness comedy and an unforgettably beautiful ending its the kind of film that sticks with you long after watching it and truly makes you care for the characters and sympathize with their pain nino rota is at the top of his game in the film and is worth mentioning here the range of emotions that his music evokes in this film alone is incredible even in the very same piece rota can seamlessly switch from a jazzy mambo to a slow orchestral sound in an instant not only is the music itself incredible though it also cant be a more perfect match for the film that it compliments the eerie and dreamlike vision of 1950s rome paired with rotas flawless score creates an atmosphere that you just get sucked into and enchanted by fellini has this incredible ability to make films about serious and painful topics simply a joy to watch you cant help but smile at the gaiety with which he approaches filmmaking and the lightness of touch that characterizes so wanna go over the topic of gaze again in my other post i defined gaze as the lens through which the author is assuming his audience is viewing his work in the western world white gaze tends to be a phrase thrown around a bit whenever we talk about not only the amount of representation in media but also in how those identities are represented a movie can be about black asian latino or indigenous societies but if the majority of the creative heads are white its going to be told from a white mans view and perception of that culture a way around this is to let underrepresented communities in western culture tell their own stories if its somehow impossible to find someone from that community to direct or write form a story trust with people from said community to give their input weve seen this done quite a bit with animated movies like moana encanto soul and coco but even if you can get an asian director writer crew and cast put together if youre aiming to release this to a western presumably nonasian audience then ostensibly youre going to conform your story in a way that makes sense to them your whitenonasian audience is going to feel a sense of lockout if your movie tv show musical etc doesnt take into account their lack of knowledge here right maybe sometimes as weve been seeing recently with media being more globalized a growingcontingent of americans want to engage with foreign cultures in an authentic way i call it the epcot effect americans want tofeel as if theyre having a genuine cultural experience but at the same time they want something that overall fits their perception of the culture theyre engaging with if there was a colombian pavillion at epcot id imagine theyd pull mostly from traditional colombian culture like pre1950s youre not going to have dudes on every corner trying to sell you watches while a group of cumbia musicians serenade a couple eats street food as they wait for a bus but by having a latino writer codirector composer and songwriter on your creative team none of whom are actually colombian you can sell your new fantastical musical to gringos as a somewhat culturally accurate and authentic experience albeit provided to you by a multinational media conglomerate studios see the value in creating content that appeal to diverse audiences and want to make the maximum amount of dollars as possible for every project theres this juggling act of making sure the community represented is appeased but also that your white audience isnt alienated by something not feeling made for them crazy rich asians feels like the endgame minutes everything else we get on top of that does not its not that im a prude but it was sometime around when margot robbie gets swallowed and embraced by this ravenous yet oddly heteronormative mass of bodies that i started asking when does the story start thats another thing for as much as chazelle attempted to show the extremity of everything its still oddly sanitized there is so little actual male homoeroticism in this movie despite it priding itself on its debauchery i actually went back and watched the opening scene and i counted how many instances of male on male i could find and it was about three plenty of girl on girl though hell lady fay basically only exists to make out with nellie and then get the fuck out of the movie yet any hint of gay intimacy or sexuality was either thrown into the background or not elaborated upon im not saying i need twinks barebacking for three hours but it plays into my overall point how a lot of the excess and maximalism is very restrained because at its root this is still a movie about how great movies are it doesnt seem very fond of the industry which is fair but it unequivocally comes down on the side of movies are magic so theres only so far its willing to go in its depictions of things like sex drugs and all the things our mothers warned us against whenever it does approach anything close to actual commentary it either shies away from exploring it deeply or tosses in something over the top and gross the best example is nellie at the rich people party where shes trying to present herself as a proper lady the scene is probably too long even without the way it ends but overall i thought it was effective nellies speech in this sequence while not the best written monologue gets the point across well shes not a part of this world and has learned she no longer aspires to be great stuff so why must we bookend the scene with her vomiting its shocking but its wholly unneeded there was a perfectly good scene with a solid arc within it chazelle wields sex and vulgarity the same way a child does after finding his dads gun there is a fascination and an amusement present but not a demonstrated understanding of what hes handling this is extremely apparent in how he treats his queer and non white characters earlier i asked what we learned about the central characters from the opening scene lets go deeper what did we learn about lady fay shes asian shes hot and shes into women whats her inner pathos wants or needs who knows we dont really ever find out later in the movie either what did we learn about sidney hes black and plays jazz we dont even learn his name until much later on we never learn much about him besides he wants to play music in the movies and be respected for his art he gets that one blackface scene and then hes out of the movie neither he nor fay truly feel like actual characters instead they feel like props created to get across the most tepid of middle of the road liberal messages hey did you guys know that the film industry was racist did you know that homophobia was bad this is funny when you take into account that chazelle genuinely was trying to create some solid representation while taking shots at the industry of the past and today the original script i read months ago gave sidney and fay a bit more to do he even had a love interest this version of the script was a lot more critical about the industrys treatment of black and asian artists and was more pointed about how this was reflected in the product we got im not in damian chazelles head so i cant tell you exactly what he was thinking but the fact that these scenes got cut only proves my point about restrained excess hes willing to show as much straight sex drug use bodily fluids and a guy eating a rat as he can but he or maybe rather the studio is less game about getting realistic about how racism has shaped the industry and the movies we love lady fay is an interesting case because the goal appears to be to show an asian woman during that time period that didnt conform to common held stereotypes i will admit she is pretty interesting shes cool tempered clearly intelligent and isnt really afraid of anything but in attempting to combat other stereotypes they fell right into another one the exotified and sexualized asian woman we get almost no real insight into her character outside of her attraction to a white woman the references that exist to her culture and background are presented in a very orientalist manner shes beautiful yet mysterious shes alluring yet theres an element of danger to her the only thing that sets her apart from actual stereotypical characters of the era is that shes queer even then we only see her flirt with women and kiss nellie the film is remarkably chaste as it regards its depictions of sexuality in general while the opening orgy has a lot of quick shots of drug fueled rabbit pumping we never get any actual sex scenes nellie and manny never do much more than kiss we never see jack with anyone the closest we get is the whale getting pissed by on a hooker the film wants to be sexy but it doesnt want to elaborate on that sexuality i dont need two hours of some white guy calling jovan adepo the n word but it is somewhat shocking to me how very little overt racism is faced by any of the major non white characters it peeps up here and there but its not focused on outside of one scene involving blackface why be subtle here you have no problem showing dead hookers all manner of bodily fluids snake bites people get lacerated yet you draw the line at showing racism with that same rawness and intensity even the blackface scene is ridiculous even when we see the footage of sidney without makeup he looks like a black man maybe the role was written with a lighter skinned black man in mind but as is i actually laughed at the scene when they show him with the makeup on its not much of a difference you can say oh were supposed to laugh at the absurdity of it but we also have eyes the basic premise doesnt work there are tons of other examples of real life racism faced by black performers in that era they couldve actually pulled from not much mention is given to the fact that sidney probably couldnt even eat at many of the places he performed there is some irony in that its the mexican man who both tells the black man to put on blackface and then fires the asian woman but the movie never really explores that while were here lets talk about manny diego calva delivers a solid enough performance for what hes given but i find how manny was handled to be interesting as well even in the script i read there wasnt much attention given to mannys ethnicity which is interesting because this script is set during a time in which there absolutely wouldve been latinos resided in a strange grey area in the american racial caste system while they werent considered outright white they werent seen as black either although this depended a lot on if the person in question was white mestizo indigenous or black delores del rio was a mexican actress who spent most of her career playing exotic types of any ethnicity but she often portrayed herself to have basque heritage and downplayed whatever other heritage she might have she was able to portray herself as a sophisticated spanish lady and while playing exotic roles rarely played the hypersexualized and racialized characters women like rita moreno would play not long after her i bring delores up because when manny mentions at one point hes actually mostly spanish it reminded me of how actual stars back then would market themselves but thats basically it it gets brought up here and there with a few sly remarks about him speaking english but otherwise its not a focus which again is weird since there is a lot you can do with the concept of a mexican man one who does not immediately read as white entering the hollywood machine at the advent of sound hollywood previously was relatively diverse for the time as the movie does show that diversity was quickly washed away as studios began to solidify what americanness was as the hayes code took effect there are traces of this transition here and there but much more attention is placed elsewhere the racial elements read as an afterthought in the final product its as if chazelle felt as if he had to include these pieces because audiences would expect it but he didnt know quite where to go with it but directly critiquing this forced homogeneity would also be taking aim at the films themselves and that would run counter to chazelles ode to films and the filmmaking process mannys relationship with nellie is interesting to me for a somewhat different reason in the script i read i remember them actually hooking up at some point they dont in the movie and the film portrays manny as someone who idolizes nellie more as a symbol of hollywood and the movie making process as a whole shes the beautiful glamorous blonde girl who seems to have been tailor made for the pictures i actually thought at one point they were going to reveal he was a closeted gay man because his slavish devotion and adoration of her is not dissimilar from the way gay men treat our divas but no manny is a boring ole straight boy who happens to be fascinated with the most toxic chick in the world i actually dont mind that they never actually become a couple because the point isnt if he gets the girl its if he realizes the girl and what she represents are no good for him theres flashes of this realization here and there but we never get a moment where manny unpacks the connection of the two in his mind but what fascinates me the most about manny is how thoroughly desexualized he is he rebuffs several womens advances he shows no interest in lady fay like most of the other men he doesnt partake in any of the coke orgy we see in the beginning one could read this as a response to the latin lover stereotype it is interesting to me how this very ethnic man lusts for this blonde waifish white woman for three hours yet he never gets more than a kiss from her sexually we only see her involved with or implied to be with white men but moreso mannys storyline just feels like a wasted opportunity he never comes to any realizations that stick on his own about the people in his life or the world he lives in his character arc is almost purely external he doesnt make the choice to leave los angeles not really hes too weak of a cinnamon roll to say no to nellie and jack and thus he gets dragged into their spheres if his ethnicity was more involved in his storyline and his disillusionment came from how he personally was treated i think the film wouldve been stronger for it the movie finally decided to have an actual plot in the last hour or so as nellie returns to manny asking for help with her gambling debts this storyline gives us nothing but the most useless depiction of shock and vulgarity chazelle has to offer manny meets with some shady loan shark played by toby mcguire who was told he was going to be in a blumhouse production if were to go by his performance chazelle channels his inner paul thomas anderson and gives us that one scene with the drug dealer in boogie nights and then decides to make the next ten minutes or so a james wan film were taken into this underground labyrinth of deformed freaks masticated men in bondage gear being whipped and giant muscle men eating rats this is all to set up that toby mcguire finds out the money theyre giving them is fake which couldve been done five different ways than how they did it without this we wouldnt get some of the freakiest and disturbing imagery in the film but to what end does it serve id argue not much it doesnt expand on any of the themes or really result in any character growth it gets manny out of los angeles but there were other ways of doing this that didnt require nearly an hour more of content the scene is vulgarity for the sake of vulgarity this might have actually been palatable if not for how chazelle chose to end the film the ending isnt actually wholly bad i picked up on the singin in the rain comparisons early on myself so manny literally watching it at the end didnt throw me much what did throw me was that ending montage that felt one of those gee arent movies great compilations we get at the oscars if you had cut that and honestly most of the flashbacks and the weird color stuff the ending wouldve worked it wouldnt have made the film a masterpiece for me but it wouldve felt complete however the ending as is speaks to as why the film treats its subject matter what it does hollywood is a seedy overindulgent machine and chews and spits people out but isnt it great that either way those same people get to be remembered for all time in the movies jean smarts monologue towards the end is basically just her saying this in not so many words but there is a distinct difference between that monologue which could almost be read as ironic and basically shooting your load over how amazing the final product in the process is lastly what strikes me about chazelles approach to this film is how old hat it feels at times on a technical level its a wonder throughout but outside of that i cant help that weve seen this before plenty of people have made comparisons to wolf of wall street and baz luhrmanns filmography but it also reminded me of ryan murphys hollywood which takes a similar approach but focuses a lot more on those who were marginalized at the time sidenote its not very good but my point is that we get these kind of projects about the industry pretty regularly we get films that actually do push boundaries as it concerns sexuality and race opulent and decadent world of 1920s hollywood peopled by characters who despite their fame and fortune live tragic lives the film begins during the set up of one of the bacchanalias offered by a hollywood studio executive and it opens with the logistical problem of transporting an elephant into the executives mansion which is atop a hill chazelle ably chronicles the excesses of the prominent would love to chop it up this essay was instigated by watching gladiator ii a profanation but it is not a review of that film this sub had seen as many of those as the day is long rather it was written in condemnation of a trend that this film raised to its most wretched and repugnant heights hollywoods aversion to the notion of closure this is not however a condemnation of the idea of sequels many of my favourite films are sequels the empire strikes back the return of the king the last crusade and others the idea of telling a story in parts is as old as storytelling itself cf the gilgamesh epic many great works of art are in parts goethes faust and manns joseph und seiner bruder come to mind heck only very recently had denis villenueve made a pretty succesfull twoparter from dune but to take my first example what is there in the relationship of the empire strikes back to star wars that is unlike the relationship of gladiator ii to gladiator or of the force awakens to return of the jedi for that matter its very simple the original star wars 1977 left the door open for sequels darth vader survives to fight another day the fate of the empire at large remains ambiguous luke has yet to wield his fathers sword in battle and theres an implicit love triangle between the heroes thats only really setup in the final reel by contrast a film like gladiator ends with a period an authentic cadence a fullstop you can make speculative what happened to this character or that after stories in your heads but the actual story the conflict of the film is concluded in the case of gladiator maximus gives his life for the cause lucila lucius and gracchus are made safe jubba and the other gladiators freed the games forfeit and rome reinstated as a republic the closing shot shows literally a rosier day shining upon the city the same can be true in a film series return of the jedi is a somewhat middling film but it is a complete resolution luke is a fullfledged jedi the emperor slain vader expires and the empire defeated this last point was implicit in the original edit and explicit in the special edition other films in this vein dont sealup every story point avengers endgame comes to mind but nevertheless build to such a crescendo that most people will percieve it as a finale once that cadential feeling is fired up it cant be unfired still other films are not concluding entries in the same sense but are clearly billed as a kind of final farewell to the characters the last crusade and toy story 3 come to mind what do all these films however have in common they all had further sequels made usually people pick on the fact that many of those sequels were made a long time afterwards that sure doesnt help in terms of actor availability or more essentially in attempting to recapture the same sensibility but thats nevertheless not the real issue that leads to so many of these films being souldcrushingly bad the issue is quite simply that theyre anticlimactic and they have to be that because they followup a film that had a complete resolution again to take the gladiator example it takes only a few minutes of gladiator ii to realize that every single thing the characters fought and suffered towards in gladiator had been dismantled lucius was no longer safe lucila and gracchus were forced into hiding people were still being enslaved into the gladiatorial arena and rome returned into the hands of cackling dictators and it only goes further south from there these are storytelling choices made by the writers but theyre ones that to some extent were inherent in making a gladiator sequel to make one you have to untie the knot of resolution that the original ended with otherwise you have no premise discounting for the moment more anthologylike film series ala star trek or indiana jones one thought experiment i like to perform is to take a film series and condense it down into one long movie surely with all the returning characters settings and callbacks thats precisely what so many of these sequels are going for they want to knit themselves right into what had come before so if we take this thought experiment how would the pair of gladiator films or the nine star wars features make sense as a viewing experience does it make sense to watch maximus go through nine circles of hell and ultimately give his life to see a reformed rome only to then have this incredibly cathartic moment doused with cold water its the equivalent of if casablanca ended lights came up and just as you were starting to get out of your seat lights came down and there was a 45 minute epilogue to the effect of and then the nazis caught laszlo kileld him ran a train on ilsa but its okay because something good came out of some other character how would that not ruin the movie beyond the storytelling aspect of it would that be a gratifying way to shape a movie its only natural for a piece of storytelling to have a crescendo and then a diminuendo as it wrapsup and concludes why then have a big crescendo if thats not actually going to be the end of the piece it would be like if sibelius 7nth kept on going for another ten minutes anyone listening would find it anticlimactic such is hollywoods aversion to finality of late that it seems that as long as a character of any sort is left standing at the end of the piece theres grounds for a sequel but finality in storytelling doesnt have to come from a gotterdamerung type of then everyone died the end kind of resolution and yet while this kind of choice would seem ridiculous to us in a single film narrativelly and structurally its somehow something were willing to accept in the case of a pair of films or a longer series were willing to accept it because we go to these films and wathc them why if the whole point of a film series of this sort is to be a larger tale told in parts then why should we be accepting of such notions why do we take a nicely wrapped gift with a bow on the top and tear it to pieces chen will never again go for this kind of aftertheending sequel again i urge you all to do the same hollywood can gorge itself on sequels as much as it wants but not of this kind opening a lot of us are familiar with forrest gump its been 30 years since it took storm and entered the public consciousness the way it is talked empire isnt going to subjugate itself part 2 extract begins mild spoilers and content warning on extreme violence felipe gálvez the settlers like the nightingale before it is unmistakeably a story about racial extermination just as in tasmania where the black wars of 182432 the indigenous population from around 2000 to fewer than 100 the tierra del fuego massacres shown here reduced the selknam population from about 4000 to under 300 in both cases around 80 of the natives were killed or died of starvation after being driven off their traditional hunting lands when roger ebert reviewed aussie film the proposition 2005 he stated that it was the closest cinematic realization he had seen to the cormac mccarthy novel blood meridian 1985 a gold standard for elegant art with a deeply pessimistic almost antihumanist philosophy and an existential western with horrific violent action the novels central thesis seems to be that america civilization in general and most likely all of the universe is built on war in both the metaphysical and absolutely physical senses the violence in mccarthys book is not simply in the action but in its extreme apocalyptic worldview the settlers more literally conforms to the action of blood meridian which follows a group of american mercenaries hired by mexican authorities to annihilate indians and bring back their scalps for bounty here brutal scot maclennan the red pig mark stanley texas bill benjamin westfall and the reluctant young halfblood or mestizo segundo camilo arancibia are hired by a rancher to do exactly the same he will pay them per ear taken from the corpse of a slain indian so how does the film compare to the mccarthy classic in a village voice review which describes the film as a revisionists revisionist western michael atkinson notes the blood meridian parallels but argues that evoking mccarthy and his most violent book is a little misleading most of what you might hear about the settlers is about its brutality but i found the movie almost strangely tasteful the violence conveyed in an artfilmsdiscreetdistance kind of way in the case of the movie he argues the sheer brutality of ethnic cleansing of handson genocide is not confronted as it is repeatedly in the nightingale and instead the film concentrates on the other strand of what makes blood meridian so popular the lyrical evocation of the beauties of a landscape as far from civilization as can be gálvez is more interested in the stark ranginess of the landscape and nailing down this time and place at once both dogmatic and engagingly eccentric the settlers does smudge its evilcolonialist through line instead of ceaseless slaughter à la mccarthy the film has a spare picaresque shape to it michael atkinson felipe gálvezs the settlers portrays genocide through an art house lens village voice january 12 2024 though the description of the film is accurate atkinson misremembers blood meridian which has a few striking set pieces of almost unbelievable brutality but is very far indeed from ceaseless slaughter in general the literary zeitgeist tends to exaggerate wildly the violence of mccarthys novel and there are much much worse around large swathes of the text are taken up by descriptions of the troop passing through meadows forests plains and deserts and revelling in the texture and particularities of these places only mexicos sunscorched desert is missing from the films exploration of landscape the central figure is similarly ambiguous in both stories cormac mccarthys kid is judged by judge holden as being uncommitted in his heart to the savagery he has undertaken along with the other indianhunters you alone were mutinous you alone reserved in your soul some corner of clemency for the heathen likewise mestizo kid segundo is judged from the beginning as an ambivalent figure by texas bill half indian half white you never know who theyre gonna shoot though bill is an uncultured cowboy with little learning unlike the tremendously erudite judge holden its noticeable that he is much given to judgement talking almost constantly about how things are supposed to be officers should have army units they shouldnt eat fish but meat they musnt leave traces and so on and so forth hes a judge with very little sense of whats really judicious just as the kid in mccarthy comes to face off against the judge but fails to kill him so too segundo on the first expedition to an indian village has a clear shot at bill but shoots wide but most driven by hate toward the kid segundos ambivalence is maclennan who rages at his judging eyes you watch me with those eyes one more time and i will extinguish your fucking flame this is followed abruptly by the kiss of death a bizarre and threatening moment and the order to go and rape the maimed native woman they hold captive so that segundo no longer has the moral high ground to judge him from clearly the theme of judgement and actions with and without judgement weigh heavy on the story and its murderous characters just as they do in blood meridian the film will play as does mccarthys book on what the ambivalent attitude of the protagonist really means we dont see segundo killing a native during the raid but he takes part in the expedition and helps the others do so he commits one killing that we see which could possibly be considered an act of mercy and later confesses to a larger number that we did he doesnt kill the killers when he has the opportunity and thus indirectly condemns the village to death segundos passive approach in the face of slaughter gains nothing for anyone just as the kids secret reservations about his murderous work changes the outcome not at all and only provokes the unending quest for vengeance from the judge meanwhile segundo is plagued by visions of a monster or god that may be his judge or his destiny narratively this film has the same spare picareseque shape as blood meridian the same terseness of dialogue and mestizo mixing of english and spanish language it even follows the exact same structure of a main narrative followed by an extended epilogue many years later the film like the novel absorbs many red room to a big white one its a lot like the one aster uses in midsommar in the lead up to the group sex scene but i know hes publicly mentioned the influence greenaway has in his work so no
